conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official eligibility criteria, and then I'll ask you some questions about your circumstances.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. I've reviewed the eligibility criteria. To help determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions.

First, do you have any children you're responsible for, and if so, how many?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child. His name is Alex, and he's 18 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Alex is 18 years old, I need to ask:

Is Alex currently in approved education or training (such as A-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs at level 3 or below, or similar courses)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment. He finished school and isn't studying anything right now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I need to ask a few more questions to complete the assessment.

Does Alex live with you in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me here in the UK. We're in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment:

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. One final question:

Is Alex currently working 24 hours or more per week, or is he receiving any benefits in his own right (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is working full-time now - he works at a local warehouse, so that's more than 24 hours a week. He doesn't receive any benefits himself though.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me provide you with my decision:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Assessment Complete**

I'm afraid you are **not eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

Here's why:

While Child Benefit can continue for young people aged 16-19, they must be in approved education or training. Since Alex is 18, has finished school, and is not currently in any approved education or training programme, he doesn't meet this requirement.

Additionally, the rules specifically state that Child Benefit stops when a child starts paid work for 24 hours or more per week and is no longer in approved education or training. As Alex is working full-time at the warehouse (more than 24 hours per week), this further confirms ineligibility.

If Alex's circumstances change in the future - for example, if he decides to return to education or approved training - you may become eligible again at that point, as long as he's still under 20 years old.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or your situation?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period; Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
circumstances: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old and not in approved education or training. According to the eligibility rules, Child Benefit normally continues for children aged 16-19 only if they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex has finished school and is not currently studying or in training, and is working full-time (24 hours or more per week), he does not meet the criteria. The rules specifically state that Child Benefit stops if a child 'starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training.' Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 18 years old. Alex is not in approved education or training and is working full-time (more than 24 hours per week). Under Child Benefit rules, eligibility for children over 16 requires them to be in approved education or training until age 20. Since Alex has left education and is working 24+ hours per week, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.
